en_body:
I walk upon the straight unending dike
Two rows of bamboo murmur—the leaves singing
And below is the river
Water dark as chocolate
The fragrance of mud entwined with rice
O my Darling!
How I miss those fingers...

I walk into the orchard
Banana trees lull their blossoms
petals opening, each tiny cluster of fruit
Jackfruits cling abundantly, trunks close to earth
A breath of pomelo fragrance, someone
 boils bồ-kết to wash her hair
To perfume your shoulder
O beautiful radiant Beloved

Your skin is like alluvium
Sweet with the lotus fragrance of June
There is a season of betrothal/asking/wedding in faithfulness
O my Darling, do not
 look at me
 and smile,
you make me shy...

I walk into the backyard
Sunlight shimmers from the heart of the cool deep well
The star-apple tree, leaves with two-colored faces
Has tiny yellow flowers I weave into a wreath
The ripened fruit wears rose-velvet skin
Cut it in half, spoon out the flesh, oh,
 translucent and cool,
O my Beloved: those are your eyes

I lie watching the moon hang shimmering
Through the window bars
The cotton blanket edged in satin
Light, soft, and comfortable

O my Beloved
Are your lips sweet tonight?
May your breath remember to utter
 one blessing of happiness
 for me!

I enter into sleep
There are thousand-winged stars
singing the diamond song
My love is like starlight
Shining back from fairy tales:

Oh, my Southern Homeland
Before April, Seventy-Five..!!
note: “Before April, Seventy-Five” evokes South Vietnam before the fall of Saigon on April 30, 1975; bồ-kết pods are traditionally boiled as a fragrant hair wash.
